sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Researchers measured hepatic fuel metabolism during fasting. Liver glycogen fell over 24 hours, gluconeogenesis increased, and ketone bodies rose markedly by 48 hours. Subjects with a defect in mitochondrial beta-oxidation developed hypoketotic hypoglycemia during fasting, despite normal glycogen stores at the beginning of the fast.
questions.stem: At 48 hours of fasting, which process is most increased according to the figure?

explanations.C: Correct. Ketone bodies rise markedly by 48 hours in the figure.
explanations.B: Insulin is low during fasting, and hepatocytes do not use GLUT4 as their main glucose transporter.
explanations.D: Dietary absorption is not the measured fasting process.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Hepatic metabolic changes during fasting.

questions.stem: Why does a beta-oxidation defect cause hypoketotic hypoglycemia?

choices.B: Fatty acid oxidation supplies acetyl-CoA and energy needed for ketogenesis and gluconeogenesis
choices.A: Beta-oxidation directly converts ketones into glycogen
choices.C: Beta-oxidation occurs only in red blood cells
choices.D: Fatty acid oxidation prevents any need for ATP

explanations.B: Correct. During fasting, beta-oxidation provides ATP/NADH and acetyl-CoA that support gluconeogenesis and ketone synthesis.
explanations.A: Beta-oxidation breaks down fatty acids, not directly ketones into glycogen.
explanations.C: Red blood cells lack mitochondria and cannot perform beta-oxidation.
explanations.D: Fatty acid oxidation generates ATP; it does not remove the need for it.

questions.stem: Which molecule is a major substrate for gluconeogenesis after prolonged fasting?

choices.A: Acetyl-CoA from fatty acids as a net glucose source

explanations.D: Correct. Alanine can be converted to pyruvate and used for gluconeogenesis.
explanations.A: Acetyl-CoA cannot be converted to net glucose in humans.
explanations.B: Cholesterol is not a major gluconeogenic substrate.
explanations.C: Oxygen supports metabolism but is not a carbon substrate for glucose synthesis.

questions.stem: Which hormone pattern best matches the fasting state?

choices.C: High insulin and high leptin only

explanations.A: Correct. Fasting lowers insulin and raises glucagon, promoting glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis, and ketogenesis.
explanations.B: This pattern is typical of feeding, not fasting.
explanations.C: Leptin is not the main acute fasting switch in this passage.

questions.stem: Red blood cells depend on glucose during fasting because they:

choices.D: Lack mitochondria and cannot oxidize fatty acids
choices.B: Store large amounts of glycogen for export

explanations.D: Correct. Mature RBCs lack mitochondria, so they rely on glycolysis for ATP and cannot use fatty acid oxidation.
explanations.B: RBCs do not maintain large glycogen stores for glucose export.
explanations.C: Ketones are not converted into insulin.

questions.passage: During exercise, skeletal muscle shifted fuel sources over time. Phosphocreatine contributed heavily in the first minute, anaerobic glycolysis rose during intense early exercise, and oxidative metabolism dominated during prolonged exercise. Lactate released by exercising muscle increased hepatic glucose production in recovery.
questions.stem: Which ATP source dominates at the very beginning of exercise?

explanations.C: Correct. The figure shows phosphocreatine has the highest early ATP contribution.
explanations.A: Oxidative metabolism rises later during prolonged exercise.
explanations.B: Erythrocytes do not oxidize ketone bodies because they lack mitochondria.
explanations.D: Glycogen synthesis stores glucose and is not the immediate ATP source in contracting muscle.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Relative ATP contribution from fuel systems during exercise.

questions.stem: Lactate released by muscle can support hepatic glucose production through the:

choices.D: Citric acid cycle operating in red blood cells

explanations.B: Correct. In the Cori cycle, lactate from anaerobic glycolysis is converted back to glucose in the liver.
explanations.A: The urea cycle disposes of nitrogen, not lactate carbon as glucose.
explanations.C: The PPP generates NADPH and ribose sugars; it is not the lactate-glucose cycle.
explanations.D: RBCs lack mitochondria and do not run the TCA cycle.

questions.stem: Why does oxidative metabolism dominate during prolonged lower-intensity exercise?

choices.A: Mitochondrial respiration yields large amounts of ATP when oxygen delivery is adequate
choices.C: Anaerobic glycolysis produces more ATP per glucose than respiration
choices.D: Lactate cannot be transported out of muscle

explanations.A: Correct. With sufficient oxygen, oxidative phosphorylation produces much more ATP per fuel molecule and supports sustained activity.
explanations.B: Phosphocreatine stores are limited and deplete early.
explanations.C: Anaerobic glycolysis yields far less ATP per glucose than respiration.
explanations.D: Lactate can be transported and used by liver and other tissues.

questions.stem: A defect in muscle glycogen phosphorylase would most impair:

choices.C: Rapid glucose-1-phosphate release from glycogen during intense exercise
choices.A: Lactate conversion to glucose in liver
choices.B: Fatty acid entry into mitochondria via carnitine

explanations.C: Correct. Glycogen phosphorylase releases glucose-1-phosphate from glycogen, supporting glycolysis during intense exercise.
explanations.B: Carnitine transport is part of fatty acid oxidation.
explanations.D: Hemoglobin oxygen binding is not catalyzed by glycogen phosphorylase.

questions.stem: Which product accumulates when anaerobic glycolysis exceeds mitochondrial oxidation?

choices.A: Urea as the direct product of pyruvate reduction

explanations.B: Correct. Pyruvate is reduced to lactate to regenerate NAD+ when glycolytic flux exceeds aerobic oxidation.
explanations.A: Urea is produced by nitrogen disposal pathways, not pyruvate reduction.
explanations.D: DNA polymerase is an enzyme, not a glycolytic product.
